For reviewers, the general code path here is as follows:

1. Users call `my_bulk_writer.create(...)` (or any of the other CRUD methods)
2. The `BulkWriter` instance holds on to the new write operation and finalizes a new `BulkWriteBatch` instance in one of two scenarios:
  a. There are 20 such pooled write operations, or
  b. The new write operation touches a document already involved in a different write operation currently queued
3. Once a `BulkWriteBatch` operation is created, it is added to a queue (`my_bulk_writer._queued_batches`), and the first domino in the sending chain is called, `_send_until_queue_is_empty()`.
4. This method, `_send_until_queue_is_empty()`, calls `_request_send()`, which blocks until all rate-limiting rules agree the batch is able to be sent. Note that we are not yet parallelized, so this properly blocks.
5. The first domino method, `_send_until_queue_is_empty()`, calls the first method that is parallelized, `_send_ batch()`. A few notes about this new method:
  a. `_send_batch()` is optionally (and automatically!) parallelized with a decorator that re-routes execution to `my_bulk_writer._executor`.
  b. `_send_batch()` routes the RPC's response to `my_bulk_writer._process_response()`, which begins invoking registered callbacks for each write in the batch. All of this work still happens in a different thread via the executor, so the main thread is not held up by any of this work.
6. Now back in `_send_until_queue_is_empty()` and thus, on the main thread, the `BulkWriter` instance checks to see if the queue is in fact empty, and, if it is not, calls itself.

These are the core ideas behind the current implementation, but a few more moving parts are added in the next PR which allow for error handling and retries.

Note: This is not strictly necessary. 

Even without your change, you can override variable assignments by passing them to the `make` cmdline, e.g.

    make DOCKER_BASE_IMAGE=ubuntu:20.04 docker

Crucially, this is different from the variable assignments of the shell, which can be used in a makefile, but normally do not override.

With your change (called conditional assignment operator), even the shell variables take precedence, so you can _also_ do:

    DOCKER_BASE_IMAGE=ubuntu:20.04 make docker

See `info make "Setting Variables"`

I do have one major concern, which is that this introduces a big chunk of automated (and opinionated) feature engineering that we should be testing very well before declaring this as a stable backend. The handler will be experimental for now, so this is fine, but we definitely want to ensure a good test coverage for all the new logic in here in a new PR.

Additionally, some components here (e.g. data type inference and parts of the feature engineering like imputing) will have overlap with new packages we are developing, at which point it may make sense to merge some of the work you've done here into them, so that all other handlers can leverage it. I'll let you know when the time comes :)

```
def format_error(error: GraphQLError) -> GraphQLFormattedError:
    """Format a GraphQL error.

    Given a GraphQLError, format it according to the rules described by the "Response
    Format, Errors" section of the GraphQL Specification.

    .. deprecated:: 3.2
       Please use ``error.formatted`` instead. Will be removed in v3.3.
    """
    if not isinstance(error, GraphQLError):
        raise TypeError("Expected a GraphQLError.")
    return error.formatted
```
so `GraphQLError` instances which are to be formatted should be `error.formatted`

> the second one, however, makes VLC player end **immediately**.
> #EXT-X-ENDLIST

See [`--player-no-close`](https://streamlink.github.io/cli.html#cmdoption-player-no-close).

[Once the stream has been fully read by Streamlink and the ringbuffer been drained](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/54136f414cf7c8f5054990d45844af0ee8dd6064/src/streamlink_cli/main.py#L359-L373), Streamlink CLI will close the player process and terminate by default. [WIth `--player-no-close`, it'll wait until the player process terminates (e.g. when the user closes it or when it closes on its own)](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/54136f414cf7c8f5054990d45844af0ee8dd6064/src/streamlink_cli/output/player.py#L294-L305).

----

Regarding the plugin itself, it looks similar to plugins/streamingproviders which are already implemented, so I guess merging it would be ok. Opening a plugin request first before submitting a pull request would be much preferred in the future though, because it avoids situations like this, where the validity of such a plugin needs to be checked after the PR was already submitted.

@ioanaif, I see that you propagated @lgray's `_UprootOpenAndRead` changes to `_UprootOpenAndReadNumpy` :heavy_check_mark:, that you added a complete suite of tests :heavy_check_mark:, and that you added `unset` sentinel-based logic to ensure that `step_size` and `steps_per_file` can't both be set :heavy_check_mark:.

I added a few things:

  * Explained the incompatibility of `step_size` and `steps_per_file` in the docstring.
  * Converted the case-handling into an `if`-`elif`-`else` chain, with nesting for `step_size`/`steps_per_file` checking versus `open_files` checking, even though this means we add useless `pass` statements, so that the logic is more readable. With a series of unconnected `if` statements, I was having trouble being 100% sure that all cases were being handled properly.
  * `unset` is a singleton; we can use it with `is`. We don't have to check it via its type (as though there could be other instances of `_Unset` around). After all, if somebody created their own `_Unset` instances (ignoring that privacy underscore) and passed it as an argument to `step_size` or `steps_per_file`, then they _have_ assigned those arguments. Using `is` instead of `isinstance` makes the logic a lot easier to read and therefore be sure that it's correct.
  * The `memory_size` helper can't convert `unset` into a numerical value, since `unset` might be a placeholder for different arguments with different defaults in different functions. `unset` has to be handled before anything gets passed to this regularizer.
  * The earliest point where `steps_per_file` can be converted into a `step_size` is in the `_get_dask_array` and `_get_dak_array` functions, after the TTree metadata of all files has been read. So `steps_per_file` has to be passed into these functions for the case in which `steps_per_file` is assigned, `step_size` is unset, and `open_files=True`.

I'd say it's good now. Any counter-counter edits?

At this point I think it is appropriate to step back and thing about what we are modeling here. The terminology here around deep and shallow is quite subtle and I think at this point misleading and confusing. I don't know when it is appropriate to call one version versus another.

`_as_config_dict_shallow`: This is enforcing the following rules:

* Skipping internal fields (This step should be gone soon once we land lifecycle methods)
* Skipping optional fields that are none
* Remapping aliases.
* All first-level values in the dictionary are preserved

So this is used in the context where one is only manipulating `keys` or the structure of the dictionary. One could argue it should be a separate function that maps old key names to new key names and return that data structure.

As it is, this function returns a dictionary that is in a weird intermediate state where the _structure_ of dictionary has changed, but the values are not serializable/directly usable. It is up to the programmer to understand this, which is asking a lot.

`_as_config_dict_deep`

Does the dictionary restructuring _and_ then processes it so that it can be consumed by the underlying configuration system. This also returns the representation that is persisted with runs and passed to the UI, etc.

Step 1: structure the keys
Step 2: process the restructured tree

So broadly I think we need to have cleaner terminology here and then structure the code to match that terminology so that our future selves understand what is going on.

Yeah, I'm wary of Mock tests. It's too easy for them to miss the actual useful parts to test.

The `integration_tests` folder contains fixtures that create real indexes to test/query against. 

For example, something like this should reproduce it, using the fixtures inside (`integration_tests/index/test_search.py`):

```python

def test_query_dataset_multi_product(index: Index,  ls5_dataset_w_children: Dataset) -> None:
    # We have indexed one nbar and one level1
    dc = Datacube(index)

    # Can we query a single product name?
    datasets = dc.find_datasets(product='ls5_nbar_scene')
    assert len(datasets) == 1

    # Can we query multiple products?
    datasets = dc.find_datasets(product=['ls5_nbar_scene', 'ls5_level1_scene'])
    assert len(datasets) == 2
```

I have comments for several few fixes but I think the interface to create and assign cases to groups could be improved. Here are some suggestions:

- Wouldn't it be easiest (for the users) if we allow to define groups with custom names (I'm sure that someone is going to ask for this feature in the future) and then assign cases to the groups?
- The 2 buttons thing is a bit confusing to me, especially if you can create a lot of groups with the same and only case in them
- It feels like you don't have 100% control on the groups using the interface. For example I've tested using the 3 demo samples: cancer_case, 643595 and 643594. If you create multiple groups with only one case (for instance cancer case).

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/28093618/105154574-f77e1280-5b09-11eb-8b16-da90cb8e4053.png)

Let's say we want to add 643595 in the same group of cancer_case: and we do it from the page of case: 643595.

This creates yet another group on cancer_case:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/28093618/105154809-33b17300-5b0a-11eb-87b4-8b6f9a1036b8.png)

What if I want to add 643594 to the same group of cancer_case and 643595 and I do it from the case page of 643594? It creates yet another group in cancer_case:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/28093618/105155236-a3276280-5b0a-11eb-80ab-ee2b516b4a06.png)

So you have to manually go to cancer_case and remove the previous group. I think all this would be fixed by creating custom group names, but perhaps I'm missing something?

Did a usage first pass, I have a few high-level pubsub design questions before I dig deeper.

It looks like we're using a channel abstraction in order to be able to piggyback off of shared publisher <--> subscriber long-polling connections, where the publisher and subscriber interfaces expose the channel concept to the application code via the channel type and deal only with a generic `PubMessage` message type that oneofs across the messages for the different channels. This then entails pass-through methods to the subinterfaces for each channel, maintaining channel --> data structure maps internally, and leaking what is essentially a transport detail (the long-polling connection) to the application level by requiring publishers/subscribers to specify the channel type. I'm wondering if there's a better way to structure this such that the application code doesn't have to worry about channels and more of this logic is encoded in the type system so we have compile-time guarantees that things are 👌 .

What if we adopted a pattern similar to gRPC's shared transport channel, generalized from managing a 1-to-1 connection to managing 1-to-n connections, where we have a transport broker concept that encapsulates the publisher <--> subscriber long-polling connections. The subscriber interface for each pubsub instance takes a shared pointer to the broker on construction, and can therefore share across different pubsub instances (e.g. separate subscriber objects for `WAIT_FOR_OBJECT_EVICTION` and `WAIT_FOR_REF_REMOVED`), where new publisher <--> subscriber connections can be registered by any of the pubsub instances.

subscribers <--> broker <--transport--> broker <--> publishers

I think there could be a few advantages here:

1. We could then expose a subscriber interface per pubsub instance, templated on the concrete message type (no need for a proto oneof message to be exposed to the application code, that could be isolated to the broker), which will give better compile-time checking of the application-level pubsub messages and will obviate the need for those passthrough methods in a top-level subscriber interface that requires a channel type to be specified.
2. I think that you'd still need a proto-level channel type enum, but I think that you could get away with specializing the subscriber with _the enum value itself_. E.g. `Subscriber<typename MessageID, typename Message, rpc::ChannelType channel_type>` could be specialized as `Subscriber<ObjectID, rpc::WaitForObjectEvictionMessage, rpc::WAIT_FOR_OBJECT_EVICTION>`, where the `channel_type` is included when interacting with the broker (e.g. subscribe, unsubscribe). Dispatch of messages received in the long-poll response by the broker will still have to use a channel --> subscriber table, but that can be populated dynamically when the subscriber is constructed: `broker_.RegisterSubscriber(channel_type, this)`.
3. The transport-level optimization of sharing long-polling connections is pushed down as an implementation detail of the communication broker concept, and is a natural place to evolve other transport-level details, like switching to a bidi streaming connection or switching to a centralized message broker.
4. Other transport-level things, like the core worker client pool and the subscriber address/port, can be moved to the broker. Publisher addresses will probably continue to be required by subscriber methods, unless we want the broker to always map worker or node IDs to concrete addresses (probably less efficient in some cases?).
5. The current process of registering a new channel will be replaced by adding a new channel type to the proto enum, templating a subscriber with a new message type and the channel type, and reusing the existing broker.

I think that you would have an analogous broker abstraction on the publisher side as well (encapsulating the cached long-polling connection, controlling the message batching), although I haven't thought it through as much since I think it should be simpler than the subscriber-side.

The cons with this broker approach, as I see them:

1. Broker concept is exposed to the application level, although what's needed to configure that concept (worker client pool, subscriber address/port, etc.) is already exposed to the application level, so I'm not sure if this is any worse?
2. A shared transport-level broker will obviously have to be thread-safe, although that shouldn't be very difficult.
3. Hidden dragons that I haven't thought of.

Lmk what you think about this approach and whether I'm missing anything! This is also something that could wait until a future PR, e.g. when I port the OBOD object location subscriptions or the GCS node/actor tables to this pubsub I'm sure that I'll have to refactor some stuff anyway.

6. There are a lot of features that are not tested right now. For example the different formats in which we accept the lr_schedule / grad clip from algorithm_config is not tested. (Basically think about the features and different variations that were added in this PR, Are they all tested?)

i think the main problem with the approach is state inconsistency in the `ServicePluginManager`.

currently, the `ServiceManager` is never really used, it's always the `ServicePluginManager`, (which btw already has a `stop_services` method that we should probably reconcile).
any deletion of service state in the `ServiceManager` base class needs to be reconciled with the underlying state of loaded plugins specs by the `ServicePluginManager`, otherwise there will be state incosnstency. for example, `list_available` of the `ServicePluginManager` uses the list of loaded plugins, rather than the list of service containers like the `ServiceManager` base class. so if we only delete the services from the `_services` dict, `list_available` of `ServicePluginManager` will still return the services.
unfortunately, there is currently a certain finality to loading plugin specs, as it reads them from the cache of entry points, and also loads them into a cached property in the `ServicePluginManager`, the `api_provider_specs`.

it would be helpful to better understand the requirements, and what the problem is exactly. is this for something that has to do with state restoring? so are we trying to work around some service state problem? do we need to fully re-load services? or is this just to remove all services that haven't been started from some state data?

The [`client_id_column` and `submission_date` fields](https://github.com/mozilla/metric-config-parser/blob/91ee9f57e795719536f99b7f12add340a942d159/metric_config_parser/data_source.py#L122-L123) are defined as optional so they can (and will be null) for some of the data source definitions. Thought about this some more. Posting some comments I made internally here for the record. 

Making manifest.file a list is probably a nonstarter in my opinion:

- Potential impact on Manifest.from_file(), Manifest.from_topdir(), Manifest.from_data(), Manifest.__init__() needs to be investigated
- Impact on west manifest subcommands needs to be investigated (this breaks west manifest --path for sure -- others?)
- Getting the syntax right needs to be fixed (comma as separator does not work without other changes as I've commented on the draft PR from Rasmussen, Torsten)
- Precisely specifying the semantics needs to be done still (what if I "git pull" and one or more of the files goes away? any other edge cases?)
- Extending west init to support this needs to be investigated (do we just allow --mf to be given multiple times?)


The impact on the API is also pretty unclear to me and it looks like there are dealbreakers:

- For example, west.manifest.Manifest.abspath is an attribute that contains the absolute path to the manifest file. With multiple, files, this seems broken.
- Would west need to have to have multiple Manifest objects in flight at any given time in this idea? That would break the west.commands.WestCommand.manifest attribute, which both built-in and extension commands rely on to investigate the workspace.

TL;DR west has the assumption that there is one and only one manifest file pretty deeply baked into some core abstractions and I think it is likely that it is too late to change this.

One more nit that I missed before, sorry, otherwise LGTM (again :sweat_smile: )

The whole point of the test is to check that TBB throws an exception during initialization in case the resource limit has reached. Since neither the TBB library nor the test itself do not control the whole system, it is hard to guarantee that the resource limitation will be reached during the library initialization. However, the test may try increasing the probability of successful test outcome by lowering the soft limit instead of increasing it or leaving it unchanged. So I suggest specifying lower number to the `limitThreads` function, e.g. 10 or even 1 in the hope that the system is not doing anything during the test run on behalf of the real user ID of the test. The limit should also be returned back by the end of the test - so we might want to have another application of RAII idiom.
What do you think?
